coredata.dc:title: Xylan derived carbon dots composite ZIF-8 and its immobilized carbon fibers membrane for fluorescence selective detection Cu2+ in real samples

coredata.dc:description: Currently, heavy metal such as Cu2+ contaminants have posed a great threat for ecological security, and the discharge of sewage containing Cu2+ into water and soil will cause damage to environment and even human health. At present, fluorescent solutions are generally used for Cu2+ detection, but the they have defects such as easy leakage, difficult storage, poor photostability and inaccurate Cu2+ detection. Herein, biomass solid-state fluorescence carbon fibers membrane detection platform (NFE-CDs) was firstly fabricated by encapsulating xylan carbon dots (CDs) into metal–organic framework (MOF)(E-CDs@ZIF-8) using in situ growth methods and electrospinning technology. NFE-CDs was ued to improved response site of Cu2+ by taking advantage of cooperative hybridization and electronic coupling effects. Owning its excellent fluorescence properties, NFE-CDs displayed excellent selectively for Cu2+ based on ‘turn-on’ fluorescence response, ultra-short fluorescence response time (<1s) and detection limit (3.48 nM) that was well below U.S. EPA safety levels. The practical applications of NFE-CDs also exhibited high specificity for Cu2+ detection in zebrafish and water samples (school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River) with the recovery rates of 97%-112%. The developed fluorescence sensing platform was utilized to predict and detect water quality and safety by monitoring Cu2+ change. This strategy demonstrates a promising biomass fluorescence platform for Cu2+ detection for water treatment research and envrionment bioanalytical applications.

head.ce:title: Xylan derived carbon dots composite ZIF-8 and its immobilized carbon fibers membrane for fluorescence selective detection Cu2+ in real samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, heavy metal such as Cu2+ contaminants have posed a great threat for ecological security, and the discharge of sewage containing Cu2+ into water and soil will cause damage to environment and even human health. At present, fluorescent solutions are generally used for Cu2+ detection, but the they have defects such as easy leakage, difficult storage, poor photostability and inaccurate Cu2+ detection. Herein, biomass solid-state fluorescence carbon fibers membrane detection platform (NFE-CDs) was firstly fabricated by encapsulating xylan carbon dots (CDs) into metal–organic framework (MOF)(E-CDs@ZIF-8) using in situ growth methods and electrospinning technology. NFE-CDs was ued to improved response site of Cu2+ by taking advantage of cooperative hybridization and electronic coupling effects. Owning its excellent fluorescence properties, NFE-CDs displayed excellent selectively for Cu2+ based on ‘turn-on’ fluorescence response, ultra-short fluorescence response time (<1s) and detection limit (3.48 nM) that was well below U.S. EPA safety levels. The practical applications of NFE-CDs also exhibited high specificity for Cu2+ detection in zebrafish and water samples (school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River) with the recovery rates of 97%-112%. The developed fluorescence sensing platform was utilized to predict and detect water quality and safety by monitoring Cu2+ change. This strategy demonstrates a promising biomass fluorescence platform for Cu2+ detection for water treatment research and envrionment bioanalytical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by heavy metal ions poses a significant threat due to their toxicity and ability to accumulate in living organisms [1–3]. Copper ion (Cu2+) as a type of heavy metal ion is crucial for the growth and development of animal and plant. Whereas, excessive intake Cu2+ could lead to metabolic disorders, decrease physiological function, and other adverse reactions that exceed the human body's tolerance [4,5]. Besides, the development of industry and agriculture has led to an excess of Cu2+ entering water, land and soil, which causing serious pollution to the ecological environment [6]. World Health Organization (WHO) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set the guideline values of Cu2+ at 2 mg/L (∼30 μM) and 1.3 mg/L (∼20 μM) in drinking water, respectively [5]. Hence, detecting Cu2+ in real water is essential for human health and environmental protection.

To date, various analytical methods, including high performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (GC–MS), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) have been used for detection Cu2+ content in real water and soil. Above methods have drawn considerable attention owning their unique advantages including high sensitivity, good selectivity [7–9]. Nevertheless, current detection methods necessitate the use of costly instruments, significant amount of space, not suitable for on-site application, regular maintenance as well as skilled personnel to operate them and so on [10–12]. Those make it challenging to implement and scale them up in real application. Therefore, it is crucial to establish rapid, in suit, real time and accurate analysis methods for qualitative and quantitative monitoring content of Cu2+ detection.

Recently, fluorescence sensors (including organic small molecule materials and inorganic nano materials) are valuable analytical and detection technology due to their high sensitivity, good selectivity, and convenient operation for detection Cu2+ [13–19]. Besides, carbon dots (CDs) as class of 0-dimensional carbon nanomaterials have drawn attention used as fluorescent sensor in the field of analysis and detection with their unique optical properties and ease of functionalization [20–24]. However, CDs synthesized using chemical reagents hinder their applications for responding metal ions due to the safety of raw materials and by-products, high cost, and complex post-processing steps. And they usually derived from petroleum-based materials, which is inconsistent with the national policy of China's ‘dual carbon’ strategy [25]. Therefore, CDs with green, low-carbon, environmentally friendly and low-cost is a key problem to be solved urgently.

Biomass-based CDs for fluorescence detection usually have several advantages, including abundant reserves, diverse types, renewability, non-toxicity, high biocompatibility and easy degradation [26–29]. Liu et al. used oxidized cellulose as the carbon source significantly improved of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs). The prepared N-CDs solution exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ with a low detection limit (LOD) of 1.14 nmol/L. In addition, vitro cytotoxicity test shown that N-CDs solution were non-toxic, making them successful as fluorescent probe for biological imaging of Vero cells [30]. Bhamore et al. used acacia seeds as carbon precursors to produce small particle sized biomass CDs solution. The chelation between the organic groups of CDs and Cu2+ was used for Cu2+ detection with a LOD of 4.3 nmol/L [31]. It was notably that xylan is a kind of rich woody biomass resources and is the main component of hemicellulose, usually shows advantages of non-toxic and harmless, renewable, biodegradable and hydrophilicity, which provides a broad application prospect for its high-value utilization [32,33]. Yang et al., [34] designed a xylan-derived CDs solution for sensitive detection of tetracycline with a detection limit of 6.49 nM. Cai et al., [35] reported a single-layered single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fabricated by nonaromatic xylan for visual detection of trace chromium (Vl) with with a detection limit of 0.10 μM. Above biomass based CDs shown good biocompatibility and high sensitivity for fluorescence detecting. However, the use of CDs solution detection presents several issues, including poor portability, susceptibility to light, heat decomposition and unstable detection.

What’s important, solid-state detection platform that consist of test strip, membrane, wood, etc., usually composite with fluorescnece solution has play important role in detection owning their good advantages of portable and stable and capable of real-time detection. It was notably that solid-state detection platform based on carbon fibers membrane using electrospinning technology has attracted extensive research attention in the fields of environmental monitoring due to its portability, stability, real-time detection, ease of manufacturing and integration with flexible devices [9,36,37]. Furthermore, electrospinning technology allows for facile modification and functionalization of carbon fibers membrane, resulting in an increased number of targets binding sites and sensing elements [38–40]. This ultimately enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of membranes towards analytes, making them ideal for analytical science and portable sensing technology applications [41,42].

Herein, we conducted a colorimetric solid-state fluorescence detection platform (NFE-CDs) for detecting Cu2+ with high sensitivity and stability based on in-situ growth of ZIF-8 loaded with xylan-based CDs (E-CDs) on the biomass carbon fibers membrane (PCL/PLA), as shown in Fig. 1 A. NFE-CDs was flexible enough to be used for real-time sensing of Cu2+ in real water samples with good recovery rates ranging from 97% to 112%. As Cu2+ concentration increased from 50 μM to 250 μM, the initially blue-colored NFE-CDs gradually transitioned to a lighter shade of blue with a corresponding increased of fluorescence signal (Fig. 1B). Besides, NFE-CDs has larger specific surface area and higher porosity, which provide more contact sites, so that Cu2+can be fixed in the fiber through chemical bonding and physical adsorption methods (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, NFE-CDs exhibited a rapid response and low detection limit of 3.48 nM. As far as we know, this is the first time to design a dual-function solid-state fluorescence sensor platform based on biomass derived E-CDs@ZIF-8/PCL-PLA electrospinning carbon fibers membrane for simultaneously efficient detection Cu2+. This study explores the synthesis strategies of environmentally friendly fluorescence detection platform, from basic materials to ideal applications. It is expected that this research will provide impetus for preparation of multifunctional materials suitable for detection heavy metal ions in polluted water, and capable of upcycling into value-added materials after saturation for environmental protection.

Xylan (95%) from birch, disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA), dimethylimidazole, zinc nitrate, hydrochloric acid, polycaprolactone (PCL), polylactic acid (PLA), 2-methylimidazole, hexafluoroisopropyl alcohol were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Zebrafish were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. with analytical reagent grade. Mung beans and dialysis bags was obtained from market in Nanjing, China. Deionized (DI) water was used in the whole research.

Xylan (0.1 g), EDTA (0.1 g) and 10 mL of deionized water were transferred into a beaker, and ultrasonic treatment was carried out until they were dissolved. Then the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon reactor (50 mL) and heated for 12 h. The final product was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in deionized water. Then it was filtered by 0.22 μm pore size membrane filters, and dialyzed by deionized water in a 500 Da dialysis bag. The obtained deep brown solution was freeze-dried, and finally the powder of E-CDs was obtained.

E-CDs aqueous solution (5 mL), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.8 g) was dissolved in methanol solution (30 mL) and (denoted as solution A), then 2-methylimidazole (1.0 g) was dissolved in another 30 mL of methanol (denoted as solution B). Solution A was slowly poured into solution B under constant agitation and left at room temperature for 24 h to form a turbid solution. Finally, it was washed three times with methanol and dried overnight in vacuum at 60°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescence carbon fibers membrane (NFE-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared biomass carbon dots E-CDs@ZIF-8 (10 mg) were dissolved in ultra-pure water and ultrasonic for 4 h to ensure uniform dispersion. Subsequently, PCL (1.2 g) and PLA (1.2 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of hexafluoroisopropyl alcohol solution and stirred for 72 h to disperse the solution until uniform electrospinning solution was formed. The solution feeding rate of electrospinning was 20 μL/min and the applied voltage was 18 kV. The relative humidity was around 40% and the temperature was hold at 29°C. Then, prepared carbon fibers membrane and E-CDs@ZIF-8 aqueous solution were put into a 50 mL Teflon reactor by using in situ hydrothermal synthesis method. After 3 h, the membrane NFE-CDs was cleaned with ultra-pure water to remove the unreacted reactants and put into a 40 °C oven to remove excess water.

The fluorescent carbon fibers membrane NFE-CDs was mixed with different common substances (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, F-, Cl-, CN–, ACO-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, SCN-, HS-, Cd2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Sn2+) to test the change of fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence spectrum change was recorded in the wavelength range of 350–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm, silt: 4 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Association constant: The association constant ka is determined from the emission intensity data following Benesi–Hildebrand equation [7]
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, F0 is the fluorescence intensity (at 400 nm) of the free NFE-CDs, F and Fmax are the fluorescence intensity (at 400 nm) upon addition of Cu2+ at the intermediate and saturation point, respectively. [m] stands for the Cu2+ concentration.

Xylan is a type of hemicellulose that is primarily derived from wood, grains, and similar sources. It is a renewable, sustainable, and biodegradable material [44]. Xylan has many aromatic rings, such as oxygen-rich groups and unsaturated bond structure, which make it an ideal precursor for production of biomass-derived CDs [9,42,45]. Herein, biomass E-CDs were prepared by using water as green solvent water, xylan and EDTA as raw materials through a hydrothermal reaction. E-CDs displayed pale yellow under sunlight and showed blue fluorescence signal under Uv–vis light (365 nm) with quantum yield (QY) of 12.43%, as shown in Fig. S1. The morphology and size distribution of E-CDs were studied by TEM and HR-TEM methods. As shown in Fig. 2
A, The TEM image of E-CDs presents uniformly dispersed spherical with an average size of 2.5 nm obtained by Image J for 150 times (Fig. S2). HR-TEM images (Fig. 2B) showed the spacing between lattice structures and fringes was 0.21 nm, which corresponding to the graphene (100) surface, indicating that E-CDs contain graphite-like structures. Meanwhile, AFM image of E-CDs (Fig. 2C) showed the height was mainly distributed in a narrow range of 3.5 nm, revealing that E-CDs was mainly composed of single graphite layer. Besides, the XRD pattern of E-CDs showed a distinct wide peak at ≈21° (Fig. S3), which can be attributed to amorphous carbon from deformation caused by heteroatomic dopants or C sp3 defects in the graphite structure. The FT-IR spectra of xylan and E-CDs were shown in Fig. S4, the relatively broad absorption band of xylan at 3409 cm−1, 2910 cm−1, 1731 cm−1 can be ascribed to bending vibration of O–H, stretching vibration of C–H and C=O, respectively. Furthermore, characteristic absorption peak located at 1038 cm−1 of xylan originated from the stretching vibrations of C-O-C in glycosidic bond and C–C stretching vibration. Meanwhile, strong absorption band of xylan at 875 cm−1 reflected the presence of β-D-glycoside bond between sugar units. And relatively broad absorption band at 1379 cm−1 and 1251 cm−1 can be belonged to C-O stretching in carboxylic acid or carboxylate and C-O in acetyl group, respectively. Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of xylan, the characteristic absorption band in 1622 cm−1 and 1311 cm−1 of E-CDs showed that the polyure aldehyde was oxidated. Meanwhile, because of the hydrolysis of glycosidic bond and fracture of C-C, peak at 1050 cm−1 intensity was abate and peak at 895 cm−1 disappeared. The disappearance of the peak at 1038 cm−1 indicated that the acetyl group has undergone hydrolysis. However, there was still weak C-H vibration absorption at 3026 cm−1, indicating the presence of partially carbonized xylan in E-CDs. Furthermore, to gain an insight into chemical composition, structure of E-CDs, the XPS analysis was further conducted. It worth noting that the O 1 s was fitted with Gausian components, and C 1 s have Lorentzian components [46,47]. The full XPS spectrum was presented in Fig. 2D showed four peaks at 285.10 eV, 399.8 eV, 531.10 eV and 1071.3 eV, indicating that the E-CDs consisted of C, O, N and Na elements, and the atomic ratios were calculated as 67.56%, 25.47%, 5.6% and 1.37%, respectively. In the high-resolution spectrum, the C1s spectra was separated into four peaks at 284.51 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.55 eV (Fig. 2E), which were allocated to C–C/CC, C–O, C–N and CO, respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. 2F) was separated into three peaks at 531.5 eV, 532.4 eV, 533.7 eV, and 534 ev were originated from CO, C–O, C–O–C and hydroxy/moisture, respectively. In N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S5a), the peaks at 400.7 eV and 402.4 eV were attributed to C-N and –NH/NH2, respectively. The Na 1s spectra (Fig. S5b) was located at 1056 eV, which attributed to the Na element in E-CDs. And the fluorescence property of E-CDs was also be investigated. The change of fluorescence emission wavelength of E-CDs under different excitation wavelength was studied. As can be seen from the Fig. S6, as the excitation wavelength increased from 298 nm to 305 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of E-CDs increased from 400 nm to 425 nm, which proving that E-CDs has excitation wavelength-dependent behavior.

Yaghi et al., reported that ZIF-8 as a kind of metal–organic skeleton (MOFs) materials [48], which have diversified structure, high thermal stability, remarkable chemical resistance to boiling alkaline water and organic solvents, large specific surface area, adjustable pores and rich functional sites. And Lin et al., proved that ZIF-8 don’t has water stable [49]. Combining above features of ZIF-8, in order to achieve precise detection of Cu2+ and form a new fluorescence sensor platform, herein, an effective strategy was designed to embed E-CDs into three-dimensional porous structures during growth of ZIF-8 to obtain high-luminescence E-CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites in-situ. The formation of E-CDs@ZIF-8 was thought to be the coordination interaction of Zn2+ with the N-containing group or terminal carboxyl group on the E-CDs surface during the growth of ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. 3 A. Under sunlight, the E-CDs@ZIF-8 was a uniformly dispersed white liquid, and under Uv–vis light (365 nm), E-CDs@ZIF-8 showed bright blue fluorescence with QY of 17.14%, which proved successful preparation of the composite fluorescence material, as shown in Fig. S7. The UV–Vis absorption spectra showed that maximum absorption peak of the material was near 315 nm (Fig. S8). Besides, the fluorescence emission spectrum of E-CDs@ZIF-8 was further investigated. From Fig. S9, as excitation wavelength increased from 298 nm to 304 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of E-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a slight redshift (from 398 nm to 401 nm). The results showed that E-CDs@ZIF-8 was excitation dependent. Further, elemental species and content of E-CDs@ZIF-8 was investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. S10. From Fig. S10a, peaks corresponding to C 1s (284.8 eV), O 1s (531.7 eV), Zn (1021.552 ev) and Na (1071.052 ev) were acquired and demonstrated relative content of C, O and Zn of E-CDs@ZIF-8, 69.4%, 17.0%, 10.22% and 3.38 %, respectively. The forms of C, O and Zn in the E-CDs@ZIF-8 were investigated by further analyzing high-resolution XPS spectra. According to Fig. S10b, the analysis illustrated four species of C 1s, called CC/C–C, C–N, C–O and CO, corresponding to 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.1 eV and 287.9 ev, respectively. According to Fig. S10c, the analysis illustrated three species of O 1s, named CO, C–O–C and C–O, corresponding to 531.7 eV, 532.1 eV and 533.9 ev, respectively. Besides, according to Fig. S10d, the analysis illustrated two species of Zn 2p, corresponding to 1021.51 eV and 1044.9.9 ev, respectively. Besides, XRD analysis also displayed characteristic diffraction peak of E-CDs and ZIF-8, indicating the preparation of E-CDs@ZIF-8, as shown in Supporting information (Fig. S11). TEM images were captured to observe the morphology of E-CDs@ZIF-8. It is worth noting that E-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a regular rhombic dodecahedral shape with uniform size distribution of 280 nm and apparently no collapse, as shown in Fig. 3B and C. Interestingly, the discretely distributed E-CDs in the outside of E-CDs@ZIF-8 are not found. In addition, the EDS element map shown in Fig. 3D showed that C, O and Zn were evenly distributed on the surface of E-CDs@ZIF-8, which proved that ZIF-8 had successfully coated E-CDs. The dispersion of Zn components indicated that E-CDs@ZIF-8 coexist and were evenly distributed in the carbon matrix. Besides, TEM images also clearly showed that E-CDs@ZIF-8 has a complete dodecahedron structure, as shown in Fig. S12. And TGA analysis showed that E-CDs@ZIF-8 has good stability (Fig. S13). As shown in Fig. S14, the FT-IR spectra of E-CDs@ZIF-8 was nearly consistent with that of pure ZIF-8, except that a characteristic small peak appears at 1655 cm−1, indicating that the carboxylic acid functional group on the surface of E-CDs coordinates with Zn2+ to act as an ‘antenna’ sensitizing ZIF-8 cationic luminescence. Above results indicating that E-CDs was encapsulated into the ZIF-8 nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of fluorescence carbon fibers membrane NFE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon fibers prepared using electrospinning technology shows advantage of large surface area and excellent biocompatible three-dimensional structure, which can provide more binding sites in the fluorescence sensor molecules and effectively improve the sensitivity and response rate [27]. In addition, by introducing functional ZIF-8 materials into the electrospinning carbon fibers, the synergistic effect can significantly improve the sensitivity of substances detection. Herein, in order to improve the binding efficiency and adsorption capacity of metal ions, biomass carbon fibers membrane (PCL/PLA as the electrospinning precursor solution) was designed and synthesized by using electrospinning technology. Then, fluorescence E-CDs@ZIF-8 were grown in situ on the surface of the membrane by using hydrothermal synthesis method, namely NFE-CDs. It was notely that E-CDs@ZIF-8 and carbon fibers were bonded by chemical bonds, which making them stable recognition sites, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The pure carbon fibers membrane (NF) was composed of many slender fibers and has uniform size distribution and morphology with 415 nm, as can be found in Fig. 4B and C. Further, the E-CDs@ZIF-8 growen evenly across the surface of each NF carbon fibers with secondary structures of raised surfaces, and the vines have a similar shape with average size of 1100 nm, as shown in Fig. 4F. The evenly distributed carbon fibers greatly improve the dispersion degree of ZIF-8 particles, which was conducive to improving the sensitivity and response speed of metal ion, as shown in Fig. 4D–E. In addition, the EDS element map shown in Figs. S15 and S16 showed that C, O, Zn elements were evenly distributed on the surface of NFE-CDs, which proved that E-CDs@ZIF-8 had successfully coated PLA/PCL carbon fibers membrane. The digital picture of the carbon fiber membrane NFE-CDs was white color with uniform sheet structure, and has a light blue fluorescence signal under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 4G and H). FTIR analysis also proved the preparation of NFE-CDs, as shown in Fig. S17. Besides, NFE-CDs membrane degradation process was well known to be segmented into three evident weight-loss phases (Fig. S18). The first stage of TGA experiment was assigned due to solvent evaporation. The second and third stages can be attributed to the pyrolysis of functional groups in the polymer and degradation of the polymer backbone. In the case of NFE-CDs, the first weight loss stage was observed up to 273◦C with a weight loss of 0.86%, can be mostly attributed to evaporation of residual water adsorbed physically on PCL/PLA carbon fibers, and the residual quantity was caculated as 52.27%. Further, the second stage began at 280 °C-380 °C can be attributed to the decomposition of the polymers functional groups with a weight loss of 46.90%. The final stage was attributed to the arbitrary degradation of the carbon skeleton in the NFE-CDs adsorbent until the speed of the NFE-CDs slowed down and the weight of the NFE-CDs began to stabilize with a weight loss of 48.06% and the final residual quantity was caculated as 4.22%.

Since E-CDs@ZIF-8 showed good fluorescence properties, the spectral properties of fluorescent carbon fibers membranes NFE-CDs and the ability to detect heavy metal ions were further investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the maximum absorption peak in UV–Vis absorption spectra of fluorescence carbon fibers membrane NFE-CDs was around 300 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength in fluorescence emission pectra was around 400 nm, with a large stokes shift of 100 nm. The large stokes shift not only reduced the influence of background excitation light on fluorescence measurement, but also avoided self-quenching phenomenon, which was beneficial to increase the sensitivity of NFE-CDs and the reliability of detection. In addition, NFE-CDs showed obvious blue fluorescence signal under ultraviolet radiation (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 5A inset image. The obvious fluorescence signal facilitated the sensitive detection of heavy metal ions. Then, the change of emission wavelength of NFE-CDs under different excitation wavelengths were also studied, as shown in Fig. 5B. It can be seen that with the increase of excitation wavelength from 298 nm to 304 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of NFE-CDs changed from 399 nm to 405 nm, which proving that NFE-CDs has slightly excitation wavelength-dependent behavior. Then, in orde to study the detection ability of NFE-CDs with Cu2+. The selectively of E-CDs and E-CDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of different amounts of Cu2+ (0 μM-250 μM), respectively was firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. S19a, the fluorescence intensity of E-CDs showed a Cu2+ concentration-dependent behavior, which enhanced gradually with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The fluorescence enhancing efficiency, F/F 0, was found to have good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ within Cu2+ concentration ranged of 50 μM-200 μM. The fitted linear regression equation was F/F 0 = 1.08 + 0.01[Cu2+], R2 = 0.98. Further, the E-CDs@ZIF-8 also was applied to detect Cu2+ and the results were displayed in Fig. S19b. As Cu2+ concentration increased from 0 μM-250 μM, the fluorescence intensity at 398 nm increased gradually. And the fluorescence intensity F/F 0 and the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 50 μM-200 μM showed a good linear relationship with F/F 0 = 1.05 + 7.62[Cu2+], R2 = 0.99. Accordingly, the E-CDs and E-CDs@ZIF-8 were proposed high sensitively for detection of Cu2+. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity changes of NFE-CDs in the presence of different amounts of Cu2+ (0 μM-250 μM) was also in investigated. As shown in Fig. 5C and Fig. S20, the NFE-CDs extract solution showed a strong fluorescence signal (FL = 2.8 × 105 a.u.). After adding Cu2+ (10 μM) solution into the system, the fluorescence signal of NFE-CDs was enhanced obviously (FL = 6.0 × 105 a.u.). When other metal ions were added, the fluorescence intensity of the system changed unsignificantly, which indicated that high selectivity and sensitivity of NFE-CDs for Cu2+ was higher than that of other common substances. Besides, competitive experiments of NFE-CDs + Cu2+ with other analystes was conducted in Fig. 5C. Under the same conditions, the addition of relatively high concentrations of interfering ions (50 μM, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cr3+, F-, Cl-, CN–, ACO-, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, SCN-, HS-, Cd2+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Sn2+) in the detection system did not produce a significant change in fluorescence intensity, and then when adding (5.0 μM) Cu2+ in the detection system, the fluorescence intensity at 390 nm was significantly enhanced, which proving that the interfering ions mentioned above do not affect the NFE-CDs for detection Cu2+. The above results indicate that the NFE-CDs has a good adsorption and binding ability for Cu2+, which can be attributed to the coordination between the hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the NFE-CDs and Cu2+. Then we studied the fluorescence behavior of different Cu2+ concentrations (0 μM-250 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of NFE-CDs. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the NFE-CDs increased with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+, which was obviously concentration-dependent, further proving the ability of NFE-CDs to detect Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 5D. In addition, under ultraviolet lamp with 365 nm, the NFE-CDs showed obvious bright blue fluorescence, which was significantly enhanced in contrast to Fig. S20 inset, proving that the NFE-CDs can be used to detect Cu2+ by colorimetric method. In addition, there was a clear linear relationship between F/F 0 and the concentration of Cu2+ (50 μM-250 μM). As can be seen from Fig. 5D, F/F 0 = 0.073[Cu2+] + 0.846, R2 = 0.993 with the detection limit of 3.48 nM in the range of 50 μM-250 μM, which lower than the most reported Cu2+ sensors (Table 1 ). Compared with existing fluorescent nano-sensors for detecting Cu2+ ions, NFE-CDs has significant advantages from material preparation methods to practical application potential. From the perspective of material preparation, xylan as the raw material for preparation CDs comes from wooden biomass resources, which have advantages such as wide sources, renewable, reusable, and green environmental protection. Compared with petroleum-based materials, this is more in line with the international strategy of 'carbon neutrality' and 'carbon peaking' and meets the requirements of green development. Secondly, the synthesis process adopts the classic hydrothermal synthesis method, which is simple and easy to operate. Then, by introducing ZIF-8, the binding sites and adsorption capacity of Cu2+ were improved. Importantly, solid-state detection platform NFE-CDs based on carbon fibers membrane using electrospinning technology, allowing for facile modification and functionalization of carbon fibers membrane, resulting in an increased number of targets binding sites and sensing elements. It also has durability, flexibility and can be used for in-situ real-time detection of Cu2+, with portability, easy storage, and operability. Hence, the fluorescence sensor NFE-CDs has excellent performance and application potential. We also studied time and fluorescence stability of NFE-CDs and NFE-CDs + Cu2+, and the results are shown in Fig. S22. It can be seen that within 10 days of the test time, the fluorescence intensity of NFE-CDs has no significant change, which proved that it has good fluorescence stability and time stability. In addition, we demonstrated the ability of the NFE-CDs to detect Cu2+. From FT-IR spectrum, the vibration peaks of the amino group and the hydroxyl group were significantly weakened, which proved the bonding between the NFE-CDs and Cu2+ (Fig. S22). Next, we infered the combiation mechanism of NFE-CDs and Cu2+. The surface of NFE-CDs was rich in amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which provideed more binding sites for Cu2+ through co-hybridization and electroncoupling effect. The binding modes mainly include chemical bond coordination and electrostatic binding, as shown in Fig. S23. In general, the coordination capacity of N-containing groups and Cu2+ was –NH2 > -NR2 > –CONH->H2O [50]. And there are a large number of NH2 groups on the surface of the prepared NFE-CDs, which made Cu2+ preferentially coordinate with the NH2 surface of NFE-CDs, increasing the splitting energy of the d orbital. The increase of Cu2+ content on the surface of NFE-CDs leads to the fluorescence enhancement effect, which could be attributed to the effective chelating fluorescence enhancement phenomenon caused by the inhibition of charge transfer process [51].

Since the fluorescence materials prepared all from biomass, which despict high biocompatibility and low toxicity, then the biocompatibility of the prepared composite materials E-CDs and NFE-CDs were studied. As can be seen from Fig. 6 A, when different concentrations (0 μM–70 μM) of E-CDs were cultured together with mung beans, mung beans showed obvious germination, with a high germination rate (Fig. S24), which proved that E-CDs had low toxicity to mung beans. When E-CDs concentration increased from 0 to 50 μM, mung beans all showed obvious rooting, germinating and growing leaves (Fig. 6B and C), while when E-CDs concentration reached 70 μM, mung beans only stayed in germination stage, and the root growth did not increase. The above results proved that a certain concentration of E-CDs had no inhibitory effect on the growth of mung beans. Besides, sfter co-cultured of L929 cells with E-CDs solution, the survival rate of the cells was still higher than 95% in different culture time, which proved the excellent cell compatibility of E-CDs, as shown in Fig. 6D. Then the biocompatibility of NFE-CDs towarded zebrafish was verified, and results showed that when NFE-CDs and zebrafish were co-cultured for 14 d, zebrafish still maintained 100% survival rate, which proved that the NFE-CDs also had good biocompatibility (Fig. 6E and F). Above results indicated that E-CDs and NFE-CDs have high biocompatibility within a certain concentration range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Cu2+ in living animal samples
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the effect of different concentration (0 μM-50 μM) of NFE-CDs on fluorescence intensity of zebrafish embryo was investigated. It can be seen that when the concentration of NFE-CDs extract increased, the green fluorescence signal of zebrafish embryo was gradually enhanced, as shown in Fig. 7 A. Moreover, when Cu2+ were added, green fluorescence signal was significantly enhanced with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+ (0 μM-50 μM), which was stronger than NFE-CDs (Fig. 7B). Fig. 7C more intuitively showed the change of fluorescence intensity. These results indicated that NFE-CDs also has high selectivity and sensitivity to exogenous Cu2+ in living animals. As shown in Fig. 7D, E and F, when adult zebrafish were co-cultured with NFE-CDs, the adult zebrafish also showed obvious green fluorescence signal, but when Cu2+ were added into the system, the green fluorescence intensity of NFE-CDs showed obvious enhancement. This result was almost consistent with the fluorescence spectral result, which proved the high sensitivity of NFE-CDs with Cu2+. Further, the fluorescence time stability of NFE-CDs+Cu2+ (10 μM) in zebrafish samples was further investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 7G, with the extension of co-culture time (0–5 h), the fluorescence intensity of zebrafish increased gradually and remained stable after 5 h. These results indicated that NFE-CDs also has high stability for detecting Cu2+ in living animals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Cu2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify the ability of NFE-CDs to detect Cu2+ in the actual water environment, school water, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River were selected for verification, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7H and I, when NFE-CDs was infiltrated with different real water samples, it showed light blue fluorescence under Uv–vis lamp (365 nm). When exogenous Cu2+ was added into real water samples, fluorescence intensity of NFE-CDs was significantly enhanced, which proved that Cu2+ can be detected in real water environment. Since NFE-CDs has excellent selectivity for Cu2+, the fluorescent quantitative reaction of NFE-CDs to Cu2+ in real water samples (school lake, Xuanwu Lake and Yangtze River) were also investigated. Further, in order to confirm the detection accuracy of NFE-CDs with Cu2+, the concentrations of Cu2+ in water samples were further determined according to fluorescence titration curve. As shown in Table 2
, the recovery rates of Cu2+ in water samples were obtained within the range of 97%-112%. These results revealed that NFE-CDs can be used as a reliable tool to quantitatively detect Cu2+ with high sensitivity, high precision accuracy and good reliability.

Herein, a strategy was presented for conducting a biomass fluorescence carbon fibers membrane as solid-state detection platform with a convex surface for detection Cu2+ in real water environment with high accuracy and sensitively. To achieve this, biomass CDs (E-CDs) was prepared by using xylan as a carbon source, and E-CDs@ZIF-8 materials were synthesized in situ. Next, in order to prepare a solid-state matrix as a platform for fluorescence detection, carbon fibers membranes (PLA/PCL) by electrospinning technology was prepared. And further through in-situ growth method, the E-CDs@ZIF-8 solution and the carbon fibers membrane were placed together in a high-pressure reactor for reaction by using one-step hydrothermal synthesis process. The as-prepared composited carbon fibers membrane (NFE-CDs) displayed remarkable blue fluorescence, making it highly sensitive and selective. As a result, NFE-CDs was used as a fluorescence sensor platform for detection Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 3.48 nM. Additionally, both E-CDs and NFE-CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and can be used to detect Cu2+ content in real-water environments with recovery rate ranging from 97% to 112%. The above results verified the feasibility of the collaborative detection of heavy metal ions of biomass solid-state fluorescence carbon fibers membrane. This strategy provides a broad application prospect for timely and effective monitoring of the changes and content of Cu2+ in organisms and water environment.
